purpose: Identify high-impact next-step research directions building on AdaCoM's findings, targeting gaps in manager capacity, process reward design, cross-agent style compatibility, and domain extension.
ranking_notes: Directions were scored on novelty (new insight vs incremental), significance (practical impact on agent deployment), testability (ease of executing decisive experiment), feasibility (resource requirements), and evidence anchor (strength of supporting paper claims). Risk-adjusted value = (significance * testability) / risk_level.
search_limitations: Directions are derived solely from the AdaCoM paper and its explicitly stated limitations/ablation results. No external literature survey was conducted beyond what the paper covers.

direction_seeds.title: Learned Process Reward Model for Context Quality
direction_seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: Current process rewards are rule-based (token penalty, redundancy, format). Ablation shows they help but the rules are heuristic. A learned reward model could capture richer context-quality signals.
direction_seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Rule-based process rewards (token count, redundancy detection) are good proxies for context management quality. This is fragile: a manager could learn to avoid long context without preserving useful information.
direction_seeds.research_question: Can a learned process reward model (trained on human judgments of context quality) improve AdaCoM over the current rule-based rewards?
direction_seeds.hypothesis: A learned reward model that scores context management quality (coverage of task constraints, information density, actionability) will produce better managers than current heuristic rules, especially for tasks where long context is genuinely necessary.
direction_seeds.proposed_mechanism: Collect human preference data on (context_before, context_after, agent_action) triples. Train a reward model (RM) using Bradley-Terry or regression. Replace rule-based process rewards with RM scores. Optionally use RM as the outcome judge too.

minimum_viable_experiment.setup: Collect 500 human preference judgments on BCP trajectories. Train a 1-2B reward model. Compare AdaCoM with RM-based PR vs rule-based PR on 2 agents (Qwen, DS). Measure Mean@3 and context quality metrics.
minimum_viable_experiment.comparison: Compare against the strongest AdaCoM baseline from the paper
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_supporting_result: AdaCoM + RM-PR achieves >5% absolute improvement over AdaCoM + rule-PR on at least 3 of 4 BCP agents.

direction_seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If RM-based PR does not outperform rule-based, it suggests that context management quality is adequately captured by the simple heuristics, or that the RM overfits to annotation artifacts.
direction_seeds.killer_objection: Collecting high-quality context-preference data is expensive and annotators may disagree on what constitutes 'good' context management.
direction_seeds.killer_result: AdaCoM + RM-PR achieves >5% absolute improvement over AdaCoM + rule-PR on at least 3 of 4 BCP agents.
direction_seeds.first_week_plan: Day 1-2: Replicate AdaCoM BCP results on 2 agents. Day 3-4: Design context-preference annotation interface; annotate 100 pilot samples. Day 5-7: Train RM prototype, integrate into GRPO training loop.
direction_seeds.expected_value: High (process rewards are a known weak spot in current RL-for-LLM pipelines)

direction_seeds.title: Adaptive Manager Selection at Inference Time
direction_seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: Cross-agent transfer works but is imperfect: DS prefers concise managers, Kimi prefers detailed ones. Style compatibility matters beyond capability. Currently you must pick one pre-trained manager per target agent.
direction_seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: A single fixed manager is optimal for a given target agent. But the paper shows DS prefers concise summaries while Kimi prefers detailed histories — suggesting the optimal strategy may vary per task or per reasoning phase within a task.
direction_seeds.research_question: Can a lightweight router select the best pre-trained context manager for each agent at inference time, based on an initial probe or few-shot interaction?
direction_seeds.hypothesis: An agent's 'style preference' for context management can be detected from its first few interaction steps (e.g., how it handles ambiguous search results, how it phrases queries). A router can match this style to the best pre-trained manager.
direction_seeds.proposed_mechanism: Train a small classifier on the first K agent steps to predict which pre-trained manager would be optimal. Maintain a pool of 3-4 diverse managers (aggressive, moderate, fidelity-preserving). Route inference-time queries to the matched manager.

minimum_viable_experiment.setup: Use the 4 trained managers from the paper (DS/Kimi/Qwen/GLM-trained). For each held-out agent (OSS, Gemini, Seed, GPT-4o-mini), extract features from first 3-5 agent steps. Train a classifier (logistic regression or small MLP) to predict which manager yields best final score. Evaluate on BCP.
minimum_viable_experiment.comparison: Compare against the strongest AdaCoM baseline from the paper
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_supporting_result: Adaptive router achieves average Mean@3 within 2% of oracular 'choose the best manager for this agent' upper bound.

direction_seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If routing does not significantly beat the single-best manager baseline, it suggests agent style is not predictable from early interactions, or that the available manager pool does not span the needed diversity.
direction_seeds.killer_objection: The first few steps may not reveal enough signal; the cost of wrong routing is a full failed rollout.
direction_seeds.killer_result: Adaptive router achieves average Mean@3 within 2% of oracular 'choose the best manager for this agent' upper bound.
direction_seeds.first_week_plan: Day 1: Extract first-5-step features from all BCP transfer experiment rollouts. Day 2-3: Train classifier; cross-validate. Day 4-5: Build inference routing prototype. Day 6-7: Full evaluation on held-out agents.
direction_seeds.expected_value: Medium-High (practical impact for deployment without per-agent training)

direction_seeds.title: Manager Scaling: Does Larger Manager = Better Context Preservation?
direction_seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: Paper explicitly acknowledges 4B manager may be capacity-limited for strong agents. The relatively small gains on GLM (8.5%) vs Kimi (95%) may partly reflect this mismatch.
direction_seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: A 4B model has sufficient representational capacity to learn optimal context management for all target agents. But the Fidelity-Reliability trade-off suggests stronger agents need higher-fidelity context — which may require larger managers.
direction_seeds.research_question: How does manager model scale (4B -> 7B -> 14B -> 70B) affect AdaCoM performance for strong vs weak target agents? Is the Fidelity-Reliability boundary shifted by larger managers?
direction_seeds.hypothesis: Larger managers will disproportionately benefit stronger agents (GLM, Qwen-class) because they can preserve higher-fidelity context without capacity bottleneck. The Fidelity-Reliability curve will shift: stronger agents may tolerate more compression with a better manager, or need less compression.
direction_seeds.proposed_mechanism: Train AdaCoM managers at increasing scales (e.g., Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-7B, Qwen3-14B, Qwen3-72B or equivalent) for 2-3 target agents spanning the capability range. Measure Mean@3, context length, and modification patterns.

minimum_viable_experiment.setup: Compare 4B vs 14B managers on 3 agents (DS, Kimi, GLM) on BCP. 3 seeds each. Same training data and hyperparameters. Report Mean@3, context-length distribution, and qualitative strategy shift.
minimum_viable_experiment.comparison: Compare against the strongest AdaCoM baseline from the paper
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_supporting_result: At 14B+, GLM achieves +15-20% relative gain (vs +8.5% at 4B), while DS only improves marginally, confirming that strong agents were bottlenecked by manager capacity.

direction_seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If scaling doesn't help strong agents more than weak agents, the bottleneck is not manager capacity but the action space expressiveness or the RL training signal.
direction_seeds.killer_objection: Larger managers are slower and more expensive — the practical advantage of AdaCoM is using a small manager. This experiment may show an uninteresting cost-performance Pareto frontier.
direction_seeds.killer_result: At 14B+, GLM achieves +15-20% relative gain (vs +8.5% at 4B), while DS only improves marginally, confirming that strong agents were bottlenecked by manager capacity.
direction_seeds.first_week_plan: Day 1-2: Set up training pipeline for 14B manager (may need more GPUs). Day 3-5: Train managers for DS and GLM. Day 6-7: Evaluate and compare strategies.
direction_seeds.risk_level: Low (scaling experiment, findings are informative regardless of outcome)
direction_seeds.expected_value: High (would resolve the paper's most obvious open question)

direction_seeds.title: Plan-Ahead Manager: Multi-Step Context Management Strategy
direction_seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: AdaCoM operates per-step: the manager modifies context before each agent step. The tiered strategy (for GLM/Qwen) shows occasional batched compression, suggesting that not every step needs intervention. But the manager still decides per-step.
direction_seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Optimal context management is reactive and per-step. But good context management may require planning ahead: knowing that in 3 steps the agent will need evidence X, so preserve it now. The per-step greedy approach may miss these dependencies.
direction_seeds.research_question: Can a plan-ahead context manager that proposes a multi-step modification schedule outperform per-step greedy AdaCoM?
direction_seeds.hypothesis: A manager that plans ahead (e.g., 'for the next K steps, I will let context grow, then compress at step N') can achieve better trade-offs between fidelity and reliability than per-step decisions, especially for tiered-management agents.
direction_seeds.proposed_mechanism: Augment the manager action space to include a 'plan horizon' field. The manager can commit to a sequence of operations (e.g., 'no change for steps t..t+3, then compress at t+4'). At the horizon, the manager re-plans. Implement via two-stage generation: first produce a plan, then generate per-step operations.

minimum_viable_experiment.setup: Start with a simple rule: for GLM-tier agents, force the manager to only compress every 5 steps instead of every step. Compare with original AdaCoM. If this simple rule already matches or exceeds original performance, the plan-ahead hypothesis has traction.
minimum_viable_experiment.comparison: Compare against the strongest AdaCoM baseline from the paper
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_supporting_result: Plan-ahead (horizon=5) achieves comparable or better Mean@3 while reducing manager invocations by 60-80%, substantially lowering inference overhead.

direction_seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If plan-ahead doesn't help or degrades performance, it suggests that reactive per-step decisions are already near-optimal, or that planning introduces prediction errors that outweigh the benefits.
direction_seeds.killer_objection: Multi-step planning increases the manager's reasoning burden and may hallucinate plans that don't match actual agent behavior.
direction_seeds.killer_result: Plan-ahead (horizon=5) achieves comparable or better Mean@3 while reducing manager invocations by 60-80%, substantially lowering inference overhead.
direction_seeds.first_week_plan: Day 1-2: Implement forced-interval modification (compress every N steps). Day 3-4: Evaluate on GLM with N=3,5,10. Day 5-7: Prototype the learned planning action space.
direction_seeds.risk_level: Medium (simple rule experiment is low risk)
direction_seeds.expected_value: Medium-High (addresses the inference overhead limitation directly)

direction_seeds.title: Style-Adaptive Manager via Prompt Conditioning
direction_seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: DS prefers concise working memory vs Kimi prefers detailed search history. This style mismatch causes transfer failures even between capability-similar agents. The paper identifies this but doesn't solve it.
direction_seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: A manager's modification style is fixed after training. But different agents or even different tasks may benefit from different styles. A single manager could learn multiple styles, conditioned on a style prompt or descriptor.
direction_seeds.research_question: Can a single AdaCoM manager be trained to exhibit multiple context management styles (e.g., 'concise' vs 'detailed') through prompt conditioning, and can the target agent's style be detected and applied automatically?
direction_seeds.hypothesis: Adding a style instruction to the manager's prompt (e.g., 'Preferred style: concise key findings and document IDs' vs 'Preferred style: detailed search history') during RL training will produce a single manager that can adopt different strategies on demand.
direction_seeds.proposed_mechanism: Augment the manager prompt with a 'style instruction' field. During RL training, randomly sample style instructions. The manager learns to produce appropriate modifications for each style. At inference, detect target agent style via few-shot interaction and select the corresponding instruction.

minimum_viable_experiment.setup: Create 2 style instructions (concise, detailed). Train a single AdaCoM manager with randomly sampled style instructions on DS (which prefers concise). Evaluate on DS under both style instructions and measure Mean@3 difference.
minimum_viable_experiment.comparison: Compare against the strongest AdaCoM baseline from the paper
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_supporting_result: Style-conditioned manager successfully produces measurably different context lengths and modification patterns (p<0.01) while maintaining or improving top-line performance.

direction_seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If style conditioning doesn't produce differentiated behavior, it suggests that modification style is emergent from RL reward signal and cannot be controlled via instruction alone.
direction_seeds.killer_objection: The manager may learn to ignore the style instruction if the reward signal consistently favors one style over another.
direction_seeds.killer_result: Style-conditioned manager successfully produces measurably different context lengths and modification patterns (p<0.01) while maintaining or improving top-line performance.
direction_seeds.first_week_plan: Day 1: Adapt manager prompt with style field. Day 2-3: Train with 2 style conditions on DS. Day 4-5: Analyze modification pattern differences. Day 6-7: Evaluate cross-condition transfer.
direction_seeds.expected_value: Medium (interesting but may face reward signal dominance issues)

direction_seeds.title: AdaCoM for Code Agents: Does Context Management Help Non-Search Tasks?
direction_seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: The paper explicitly limits evaluation to knowledge-intensive search tasks and calls out code agents, embodied agents, and long-form writing as untested. The structure of useful context in code tasks differs (function definitions, import graphs, error stacks).
direction_seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: AdaCoM's findings (Fidelity-Reliability trade-off, tiered vs eager strategies) generalize to code agent tasks. But code agents may have different failure modes (syntax errors, import resolution, API misuse) and different context structure (structured code vs free-text snippets).
direction_seeds.research_question: Does AdaCoM-style context management improve code agents (e.g., SWE-bench, HumanEval tasks with multi-file edits)? What management strategies emerge for code tasks?
direction_seeds.hypothesis: Code agents will benefit from AdaCoM, but the optimal strategy will differ: stronger code agents may need high-fidelity preservation of import graphs and function signatures, while weaker ones may need aggressive distilling of error stacks to avoid confusion loops.
direction_seeds.proposed_mechanism: Apply AdaCoM to code agents on SWE-bench or similar. Adapt the action space to handle structured code blocks (preserve function signatures, import statements). Add code-specific process rewards (import resolution success, syntax validity).

minimum_viable_experiment.setup: Take 50 SWE-bench instances, apply AdaCoM (4B manager + GRPO) to a code agent (e.g., SWE-agent or similar). Compare with vanilla ReAct and summarization baseline. Report pass@1.
minimum_viable_experiment.comparison: Compare against the strongest AdaCoM baseline from the paper
minimum_viable_experiment.expected_supporting_result: 10%+ relative improvement on SWE-bench for the weakest agent, with identifiable management strategies (preserve imports + function signatures, compress error stacks).

direction_seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If AdaCoM does not help, it suggests code agents benefit from a fundamentally different context structure (e.g., structured file trees, not conversation threading).
direction_seeds.killer_objection: SWE-bench instances are typically shorter (< 10 steps) than BCP (35 steps), so context degradation may be less of a bottleneck for code agents.
direction_seeds.killer_result: 10%+ relative improvement on SWE-bench for the weakest agent, with identifiable management strategies (preserve imports + function signatures, compress error stacks).
direction_seeds.first_week_plan: Day 1: Set up SWE-bench evaluation pipeline. Day 2-3: Run vanilla ReAct baseline (2-3 agents). Day 4-5: Implement minimal AdaCoM for code agents. Day 6-7: Evaluate.
direction_seeds.risk_level: Medium-High (code agents may require non-trivial adaptation of the action space)
direction_seeds.expected_value: High (would significantly expand the scope and impact of AdaCoM)
